content.text: A CSS selector is the pattern that identifies which HTML elements a rule applies to. Selectors are the first thing you write before the curly braces. Understanding selector syntax — and how browsers resolve conflicts between rules — is foundational to writing predictable CSS.

attrs.alt: CSS Selector Reference — categories, combinators, and specificity

content.text: The three most commonly used selectors are the type selector (element name), the class selector, and the ID selector.
Type selectors are low-specificity — good for defaults and resets
Class selectors are the primary styling hook in modern CSS
ID selectors have high specificity — avoid them for styling; reserve for JavaScript hooks or fragment links
The universal selector * is often used in CSS resets (*, *::before, *::after)
Attribute selectors match elements based on the presence or value of their attributes. They are especially useful for styling form elements and links.
class contains "btn" as a space-separated word
Combinators express relationships between elements. They are more specific than a single selector and let you target elements based on their position in the DOM tree.
Descendant combinator (space) is the most common — matches anywhere in the subtree
Child combinator (>) is stricter — only direct children, useful to avoid deep leakage
Adjacent sibling (+) is handy for typography (e.g., remove top margin from the first paragraph after a heading)
General sibling (~) is less common but useful for toggle patterns
Pseudo-classes match elements based on their state, user interaction, or structural position — conditions that cannot be expressed with attributes or class names alone.
Always style :focus-visible — removing outlines entirely breaks keyboard navigation for users who rely on it.
Modern CSS adds powerful logical selectors that reduce repetition and improve maintainability:
:is() takes the specificity of its most specific argument
:where() always has zero specificity — useful for base styles that are easy to override
:has() is supported in all modern browsers as of late 2023; avoid for IE11 targets
:not() accepts complex selectors in modern CSS: li:not(.active, .disabled)
Pseudo-elements create virtual elements that do not exist in the HTML source. They are written with double colons (::) to distinguish them from pseudo-classes.
::before and ::after require a non-empty content property to render (use content: "" for decorative elements)
Generated content (::before / ::after) is not in the DOM and is not accessible to screen readers — use for decoration only
::placeholder is well-supported but prefixed in older browsers (:-ms-input-placeholder)
::marker is supported in all modern browsers and lets you style bullets without resetting list-style
When two rules target the same element and property, the browser must choose one. Specificity is the scoring system that determines the winner.
Specificity is expressed as four values (a, b, c, d) compared left-to-right:
Equal specificity: the last rule in source order wins (the cascade)
Avoid !important — it opts out of the cascade and creates maintenance problems
:is() and :not() carry the specificity of their most specific argument
:where() always contributes 0,0,0,0 — useful for low-specificity utility layers
Inline styles (specificity 1,0,0,0) beat all stylesheet rules except !important

Angular components have style encapsulation enabled by default (ViewEncapsulation.Emulated). This adds unique attribute selectors (like [_ngcontent-abc]) to all component styles, preventing leakage into child components.

::ng-deep pierces the ViewEncapsulation boundary so a parent component can style elements inside a child component. It is deprecated but still widely used with third-party libraries where you cannot edit the child's CSS:
Always combine ::ng-deep with :host to prevent truly global leakage
Prefer exposing CSS custom properties (variables) in child components instead of ng-deep
::ng-deep will eventually be removed — avoid it in new code where possible
The browser DevTools console accepts document.querySelectorAll() to test selectors against the live DOM before writing CSS:

// DevTools console
document.querySelectorAll('li:not(.active)');     // returns NodeList
document.querySelector('form:has(:invalid)');     // returns first match or null
document.querySelectorAll('[data-testid]');       // find all test hooks
Overly specific rules: .page .sidebar .widget ul li a — hard to override and brittle to DOM changes
ID selectors in CSS: #header styles are almost impossible to override without !important
Descendant selectors that are too broad: .container p styles every paragraph inside, including nested components
Missing :focus-visible — stripping outlines breaks keyboard accessibility
Confusing :nth-child vs :nth-of-type — :nth-child(2) counts all siblings, not just the matching type
Using ::ng-deep without :host — styles bleed globally out of the component's scope

Browsers match CSS selectors right-to-left — starting with the rightmost part (the key selector) and working left. This means the key selector is the primary bottleneck for style recalculation performance.
Simple class lookup — engine checks one attribute
Key selector (.nav-item) is a class; ancestor check is cheap
Universal key selector — checks every element on every recalc

Deep descendant chain; every

must walk up 3 ancestors
Keep selectors short — one to three parts is usually sufficient
Avoid qualifying class selectors with a type: .btn is faster than button.btn
Do not use the universal selector in a descendant position (div *) — it creates an expensive pass over all descendant nodes
Selector performance is rarely a real bottleneck in modern browsers — correctness and maintainability matter more

CSS Cascade Layers (@layer), introduced in 2022, let you explicitly control the order in which groups of styles are applied, independently of specificity. This solves the 'specificity arms race' problem in large codebases.
A rule inside an @layer has lower precedence than an unlayered rule with the same specificity, regardless of source order. Unlayered styles always beat layered styles.
@layer is the recommended pattern for third-party CSS — import it inside a layer to prevent it from overriding your styles
CSS frameworks like Tailwind v4 use @layer internally
Supported in all modern browsers since 2022
The order of @layer declarations is what matters — not where styles appear in the file
A practical pattern for Angular reactive forms uses CSS selectors with Angular's attribute bindings to reflect validation state without adding conditional class logic in the component:
@if (emailCtrl.errors?.['required'] && emailCtrl.touched) { Email is required. } @else if (emailCtrl.errors?.['email'] && emailCtrl.touched) { Enter a valid email address. }
This pattern uses attribute selectors and :focus-within instead of adding CSS classes in the component class, keeping the logic in CSS where it belongs.
All paragraphs after h2 in same parent

CSS selectors form the backbone of how styles are applied to HTML. The core categories are basic selectors (type, class, ID, attribute), combinators (descendant, child, sibling), pseudo-classes (interaction states, structural, and logical), and pseudo-elements (generated content). Specificity governs which rule wins when two selectors target the same property — higher specificity wins, with source order as a tiebreaker. In Angular, ViewEncapsulation.Emulated scopes component styles automatically; :host, :host-context, and (cautiously) ::ng-deep provide escape hatches when needed.
